senses.definition: Sexual activities or feelings where a person gives sexual pleasure only to themself.
examples: The therapist discussed different autoerotisms in a clinical, non‑judgmental way.
Some people feel embarrassed talking about their autoerotisms with a doctor.

senses.definition: A small, simple motor vehicle, often an early or lightweight car for one or two people.
examples: The museum displayed an early autoette from the 1910s.
He collected rare vehicles, including a restored autoette.

senses.definition: The process by which a system increases its own activity without an outside cause, especially in physics or engineering.
examples: Autoexcitation in the circuit led to unstable oscillations.
The model explains how autoexcitation can start in the plasma under certain conditions.

senses.definition: A camera setting that automatically chooses the right exposure for a photo.
examples: Switch to autoexposure if you are not sure how to set the camera manually.
In low light, autoexposure may make the image too bright or too dark.

senses.definition: Different photos or measurements taken using an automatic exposure setting.
examples: The camera logged all the autoexposures during the test shoot.
Comparing the autoexposures helped her choose the best lighting setup.

senses.definition: Self-fertilization in a plant or organism, using its own sex cells.
examples: Autofecundation allows the plant to reproduce even when pollinators are absent.
The species shows a high rate of autofecundation under stress.

senses.definition: Fermentation that happens by itself, driven by microorganisms already present in the material.
examples: Autofermentation of the mash produced gas and heat.
The food waste began autofermentation after a few days in the tank.

senses.definition: A sudden increase in brightness or activity in an automatic system, especially in astronomy or optics.
examples: The telescope recorded an autoflare in the distant radio source.
An unexpected autoflare caused noise in the detector readings.

senses.definition: Repeated sudden increases in brightness or activity in an automatic or self-driven system.
examples: Several autoflares were observed during the monitoring period.
The instrument log shows multiple autoflares over the last hour.

senses.definition: Natural light given off by cells or tissues when they are excited by certain types of light, without any added dye.
examples: Autofluorescence can interfere with some imaging techniques in microscopy.
The researchers measured tissue autofluorescence as a diagnostic marker.

senses.definition: A camera feature that automatically makes the image sharp.
examples: The camera’s autofocus works well in low light.
Turn off autofocus if you want full manual control.

senses.definition: To adjust focus automatically to make an image sharp.
examples: Modern smartphones autofocus quickly on faces.
The lens struggled to autofocus in the dark room.

senses.definition: Third person singular form of ‘autofocus’: adjusts focus automatically to make an image sharp.
examples: The camera quickly autofocuses when you half-press the shutter.
It autofocuses accurately, even on moving subjects.

senses.definition: The process of forming or developing by itself without outside control.
examples: The theory describes the autoformation of patterns in the system.
Crystal autoformation occurred under constant temperature and pressure.

senses.definition: A process of strengthening a metal tube or cylinder by pressurizing it so that the inner layers are permanently stretched.
examples: Autofrettage is used to increase the life of gun barrels.
The engineer recommended autofrettage to reduce the risk of cracking.

senses.definition: Involving or relating to self-fertilization in plants or organisms.
examples: The species is highly autogamic under drought conditions.
Autogamic plants can produce seeds without cross-pollination.

senses.definition: Instances or types of self-fertilization in plants or organisms.
examples: Different autogamies have been described in unicellular organisms.
The paper compares autogamies observed in several plant families.

senses.definition: Capable of or characterized by self-fertilization.
examples: Autogamous species can reproduce in isolated conditions.
In autogamous plants, pollen from the same flower fertilizes the ovules.

senses.definition: Self-fertilization, especially when a flower uses its own pollen to fertilize its ovules.
examples: Autogamy ensures seed production when pollinators are scarce.
The plant shows a mix of autogamy and cross-pollination.

senses.definition: A gauge or measuring device that operates or records measurements automatically.
examples: An autogauge monitors the pressure inside the tank.
The line used an autogauge to check each part for defects.

senses.definition: Produced from within itself, without an outside source.
examples: The theory suggested an autogeneal origin of the structure.
Autogeneal processes may explain the formation of the deposit.

senses.definition: Different instances or forms of origin or development from within, without outside causes.
examples: Several autogeneses have been proposed to explain the phenomenon.
The article reviews competing autogeneses suggested by earlier researchers.

senses.definition: Origin or development that happens from within an organism or system, without an outside cause.
examples: The idea of autogenesis has been debated in evolutionary biology.
Some geologists argue for autogenesis in the formation of these minerals.

senses.definition: Relating to something that arises or develops from within itself.
examples: Autogenetic theories of language focus on internal cognitive development.
The rock shows autogenetic growth of crystals.

senses.definition: In a way that arises or develops from within itself.
examples: The structure formed autogenetically, without external input.
The crystals grew autogenetically during cooling.

senses.definition: Produced from within the body or system, not introduced from outside.
examples: Autogenic training teaches people to relax by using self-suggestions.
Autogenic processes in the soil affect its structure over time.

senses.definition: A relaxation method where a person uses repeated phrases and mental focus to create a feeling of calm in their body.
examples: She practices autogenics to manage stress and anxiety.
Autogenics can help reduce muscle tension and improve sleep.

senses.definition: Different cases or forms of origin from within, without an outside cause.
examples: Various autogenies have been suggested to explain the phenomenon.
The book surveys autogenies across several philosophical traditions.

senses.definition: Produced from within; growing or developing without an outside source.
examples: An autogenous bone graft uses the patient’s own bone tissue.
Autogenous processes shape the landscape over geological time.

senses.definition: In a way that is produced from within, without an outside source.
examples: The tissue regenerated autogenously after the procedure.
The system seems to evolve autogenously over time.

senses.definition: Produced from within itself; not coming from an outside source.
examples: Autogenuous explanations focus on internal causes.
The organism has an autogenuous supply of certain nutrients.

senses.definition: Origin or development from within an organism or system itself.
examples: The idea of autogeny was important in early theories of life.
Some researchers argue that consciousness arises by autogeny.

senses.definition: An aircraft with a freely turning rotor that gives lift and a separate engine that pushes it forward.
examples: The pilot demonstrated how an autogiro can land in a short distance.
Early autogiro designs helped pave the way for helicopters.

senses.definition: Aircraft that have freely turning rotors for lift and engines for forward thrust.
examples: Autogiros were used for short takeoff and landing experiments.
Modern enthusiasts still build and fly home-made autogiros.

senses.definition: Knowledge or understanding of oneself.
examples: Philosophers have long valued autognosis as a path to wisdom.
Therapy can support autognosis and personal growth.

senses.definition: Relating to knowledge or understanding of oneself.
examples: Autognostic insight is central to many spiritual traditions.
The exercises are designed to promote autognostic reflection.

senses.definition: Tissue that is taken from one part of a person’s body and moved to another part of the same person’s body.
examples: The surgeon used an autograft to repair the damaged ligament.
Using an autograft reduces the risk of immune rejection.

senses.definition: Having tissue that has been taken from another part of the same person’s body and transplanted.
examples: The autografted tissue healed faster than expected.
An autografted tendon was used to stabilize the joint.

senses.definition: The act of transplanting tissue from one part of a person’s body to another part of the same person’s body.
examples: Autografting is common in reconstructive surgery.
The risks of autografting include pain at the donor site.

senses.definition: Pieces of tissue taken from one part of a person’s body and moved to another part of the same person’s body.
examples: Bone autografts are widely used in orthopedic surgery.
Unlike donor tissue, autografts carry no risk of disease transmission.

senses.definition: Writing made by a person’s own hand; a handwritten signature or note.
examples: The document bears the king’s autogram at the bottom.
She treasured the author’s brief autogram inside the book.

senses.definition: A famous person’s signature, especially when someone collects it.
examples: The fan asked the singer for her autograph.
He keeps all his autographs in a special album.

senses.definition: To sign your name, especially as a famous person giving your signature to someone.
examples: The author autographed copies of her new novel.
He autographed the football for a young fan.

senses.definition: Relating to an autograph or written in the author’s own hand.
examples: The letter is of special interest because of its autographal notes.
The library houses several autographal manuscripts from the period.

senses.definition: Signed by a famous person or by the author.
examples: She proudly showed her autographed poster.
An autographed copy of the book sold for a high price.

senses.definition: A person who writes or signs in their own hand; sometimes, a person known for giving autographs.
examples: The famous autographer drew a small sketch next to his signature.
Collectors waited in line to meet the autographer after the talk.

senses.definition: Written in the author’s own hand or relating to autographs.
examples: The museum displays autographic letters from the poet.
Autographic evidence confirmed that the note was genuine.

senses.definition: Relating to writing or signatures done by the author’s own hand.
examples: The archive contains several autographical documents from that era.
We studied the autographical variations across the drafts.

senses.definition: In a way that is written or signed in the author’s own hand.
examples: The letter is autographically dated and signed.
The pages were autographically corrected by the composer.

senses.definition: Pieces of writing or signatures made in a person’s own hand.
examples: The collection includes several autographies of important historical figures.
Her desk drawer was full of autographies from visiting writers.

senses.definition: The act of signing your name, especially as a famous person signing for fans.
examples: Autographing can be tiring after a long performance.
The band spent an hour on autographing after the show.

senses.definition: The practice or habit of collecting or giving autographs.
examples: Autographism became popular with the rise of celebrity culture.
The book explores the history of autographism and fan behavior.
